_meta.purpose: Upgrade 28-q MBTI test from binary A/B to 4-option Likert so users feel a stronger 'test' sense and can express degree, not just direction.
_meta.structure: 4 dimensions (EI/NS/TF/JP) x 7 questions = 28. Each question = 1 scenario + 4 options mapping to [Strong X, Slight X, Slight Y, Strong Y] where X/Y are the two poles of that dimension.

option_keys.A: Strong pole X (e.g. Strongly Extraverted) — weight 2
option_keys.B: Slight pole X (e.g. Slightly Extraverted) — weight 1
option_keys.C: Slight pole Y (e.g. Slightly Introverted) — weight 1
option_keys.D: Strong pole Y (e.g. Strongly Introverted) — weight 2

scoring.mechanic: Sum weights per pole across the 7 questions of each dimension. Pole with larger sum wins; the absolute difference |X - Y| maps to strength.
scoring.weights: A = +2 to pole X; B = +1 to pole X; C = +1 to pole Y; D = +2 to pole Y
scoring.per_dimension_pole_range: 0..14 each; X+Y always = 14 if all 7 answered

strength_bands.mild: winner pole score 7 (i.e. 50%, only reachable on a 7-7 tie broken by tiebreak) — |X-Y| in 0..3

tiebreak.rule: If a dimension is tied 7-7 (only possible when answers split as 1x Strong + 2x Slight on each side, or 3x Slight-Strong mixes that net equal), fall back to the dominant cognitive-function axis already implied by the OTHER three dimensions. Concretely: tiebreak order = attitude (EI) -> perceiving (NS) -> judging (TF) -> lifestyle (JP). For the tied dimension, pick the pole whose letter leads its dichotomy in DICHOTOMIES order (E over I, N over S, T over F, J over P) UNLESS the user's already-decided dominant function suggests otherwise. In practice this resolves to: EI tie -> E; NS tie -> N; TF tie -> T; JP tie -> J. This is the same A-pole-wins rule the binary version used.
tiebreak.rationale: Keeps behaviour identical to the existing binary scoreQuiz on the boundary, so old test users see no personality flip.

_meta.tie_strength_summary: Final result = 4-letter type + one of {strong, moderate, mild} computed from the AVERAGE of per-dimension |X-Y| gaps. Avg gap >= 6 -> strong; 3..5.99 -> moderate; < 3 -> mild. This gives a single honest strength label rather than 4 separate ones.
design_principles: Cognitive-function rooted, not stereotype-rooted: scenarios are concrete situations (holding a crystal, guided meditation, picking a stone for a friend, reading a tarot deck), not abstract trait claims.
Strength is in the option wording, not a separate slider: A/D options commit visibly harder than B/C, so the user themselves decides how strongly they lean.
Avoid value-laden language: no option reads as 'better'. Each pole has its own dignity.

Reverse-coded options kept to a minimum: in this design X is always A/B and Y is always C/D for every question, so there is no key-inversion cognitive load. (Trade-off accepted: a small order-effect bias. Mitigated by varying which pole is socially 'desirable' across questions.)
All English, all ASCII-safe text (CJK is in surrounding UI strings, not here).

compat.data_layer_field: DATA.quiz (replaces the binary QUIZ constant in generate.js)
compat.field_shape_change: binary had {id, dim, a, b, q, optA, optB}; 4-opt has {id, dim, poles:[X,Y], q, options:[{key,label,score:{pole:weight}} x4]}. scoreQuiz and renderTestQuestion in generate.js MUST be rewritten to consume the new shape.
compat.backward_compat: none — this is a deliberate break. Old deep links to ?type=INTJ still work because the result-type string format is unchanged.

EI.cognitive_axis: energy direction — where attention naturally flows, outward to people and motion or inward to thought and solitude

NS.cognitive_axis: information intake — patterns, meanings, future possibilities vs concrete details, present reality, lived texture

TF.cognitive_axis: decision criteria — principle, consistency, fairness vs harmony, relational impact, felt values

JP.cognitive_axis: outer-world stance — closure, structure, planned sequence vs openness, options, responsive flow

questions.scenario: After a long, loud week, the thought that genuinely refills your cup is —

options.label: a dinner with two or three close friends, talking late into the night — I can already feel the energy coming back
a small hangout sounds good, but only for an hour or two before I want my own space
a quiet evening alone sounds good, but I would not say no to one short call
a quiet evening alone — a book, soft music, no one needing anything from me

questions.scenario: You are holding a new crystal at a market stall. Your instinct is to —

options.label: strike up a conversation with the seller right away — where it was mined, who else has held it
ask one or two questions out loud, then decide
sit with it quietly for a minute, maybe mention a brief thanks to the seller
sit with it silently in your palm for a full minute before saying a word to anyone

questions.scenario: At a gathering where you know almost no one, an hour in you are usually —

options.label: wired and unable to wind down — you have been talking for an hour straight and cannot seem to stop
loudly in the mix, though part of you knows you will crash hard on the walk home
polite and present, glancing at the door now and then
polite but ready for the car — it has been enough

questions.scenario: A big idea lands in your head. The first thing you want to do is —

options.label: say it out loud to someone straight away — it only becomes real once it is spoken
mention it to whoever is around, then keep refining it with them
sit with it for an hour or two before mentioning it
sit with it privately until it has shape — maybe for days, maybe until someone asks the right question

questions.scenario: Looking honestly at your friendships, the truer description is —

options.label: a wide circle — but if I am honest, most of them do not really know me
a healthy mix on paper — though I sometimes realise I have not checked on the close ones in too long
a small inner circle plus a few acquaintances I do not chase
a deep few — I would rather know three people down to the bone

questions.scenario: You have been alone with your thoughts for most of the day. By evening you feel —

options.label: decidedly under-stimulated — you want voices, motion, company, anything with a pulse
a touch restless — a short call or a walk somewhere with people would settle it
mostly fine — solitude is easy, though you might text one person
rested and clear — solitude is where you recharge, full stop

questions.scenario: When something difficult happens, your first move is usually to —

options.label: talk it through with someone immediately — out loud is how you process
send a voice note or short message to someone, then see how it lands
sit with it alone for a while, then maybe bring it up later
retreat and feel it through on your own first — you will share it only once it has settled

questions.scenario: A friend describes their new job. What you actually tune into first is —

options.label: what it could become in two years — though I catch myself skipping past what the job actually is today
the direction it points — sometimes before I have heard what the work even is
the basic facts — pay, hours, who they report to — before the bigger picture
the concrete details — the salary, the hours, the commute, the team size

questions.scenario: You pick up a tarot deck you have never seen. You want to understand it through —

options.label: the symbolic system underneath — the patterns, the archetypes, the through-line across the whole deck
the bigger meanings the cards point to, even before I know each one
what each card plainly pictures, with a quick glance at the booklet
what each card plainly pictures — the figures, the colours, the numbers, exactly as drawn

questions.scenario: Someone gives you directions to their house. You prefer —

options.label: a rough sketch of the route — landmarks and the general shape is plenty
the gist plus one or two key landmarks to anchor it
the main turns written down, plus a landmark or two as backup
step-by-step turns with exact street names and distances

questions.scenario: In a guided meditation, your mind naturally drifts toward —

options.label: a web of associations — what the image reminds you of, where it leads, what it might mean
the wider feelings and themes the guidance stirs up
the texture of the moment, with the occasional thought drifting past
the texture of the moment — the breath, the room, the physical sensation, exactly as it is

questions.scenario: You are choosing a crystal for a friend. You tend to pick by —

options.label: what it symbolises and what they are becoming — the stone as a marker of a chapter
the meaning behind it, loosely held
what it visibly is — the colour they love, the weight — plus a quick check on its meaning
what it visibly is — the colour they love, the weight in the hand, the surface they will run a thumb over

questions.scenario: When a problem lands at work, your instinct is to —

options.label: reframe it — maybe it is the wrong problem entirely; the framing is the real lever
ask what the problem is really pointing at before solving it
check what we already know works and start from there
look at what has worked before and do more of that

questions.scenario: Reading a book, the parts that stay with you are —

options.label: the underlying ideas — though half the time I could not tell you what actually happened in the story
the patterns and the bigger argument — I sometimes skim past the scenes themselves to reach them
the vivid scenes, plus a sense of what the book was really about
the vivid scenes — the things you could picture clearly, almost replay

questions.scenario: A friend is upset and asks for your read on a conflict. You lead with —

options.label: the principle of the thing, plainly stated — even when I can see their face fall as I say it
the principle first, then a stilted, too-late note about how everyone is feeling
how everyone is feeling first, then the principle as a quiet anchor
how everyone in the room is actually feeling — the principle can wait

questions.scenario: You have to give honest feedback that will sting. You —

options.label: say it straight, because dancing around it is a form of dishonesty
say it clearly but soften the edges once
find a kinder angle and deliver it warm, with the relationship in mind
find the kindest true version, because the relationship matters as much as the point

questions.scenario: Picking a stone for yourself, the deciding factor is usually —

options.label: a clear reason — what it is traditionally used for, what it does, full stop
a clear reason, with a small nod to how drawn I feel to it
a felt yes, with a quick check that the traditional use fits
a felt yes — something in you simply leans toward it and that is enough

questions.scenario: A decision has to be made for the group. Your instinct is —

options.label: lay out the pros and cons, then pick the option that holds up under scrutiny
weigh pros and cons, then briefly check the room
check in with everyone first, then weigh the options
check in with everyone first — a choice people can live with beats a perfect one on paper

questions.scenario: When someone is venting at you, your inner first response is —

options.label: to spot the flaw in their reasoning — there is usually one and it is the actual lever
to notice where their logic slipped, while still listening
to feel the weight of what they are carrying, while half-noticing the logic
to feel the weight of what they are carrying with them — the logic can wait until they are steady

questions.scenario: You would rather be seen as —

options.label: fair, even when fairness costs someone their preference — and I will not pretend otherwise to soften it
fair-minded, though I have been told I can land as cold when I lead with it
kind-hearted, with room for the hard truth
kind, even when kindness softens a hard truth

questions.scenario: A long-held value of yours collides with a friend's need. You are more likely to —

options.label: hold the value — bending it once means bending it always, and they would understand
hold the value, but find a small way to soften the cost to them
make some room for them, while keeping the value mostly intact
make room for the friend — values are for people, not the other way around

questions.scenario: A free weekend with no plans, by Sunday night you usually feel —

options.label: restless and oddly tired — the openness itself became work
fine but faintly itchy — at least one anchor would have helped
mostly replenished, with maybe one small wish that I had planned a thing or two
deeply replenished — the openness was the whole point

questions.scenario: Booking a trip, your tendency is —

options.label: the itinerary locked weeks ahead — and honestly, a plan that changes after that rattles me for the rest of the trip
the flights and first two nights locked, and quietly stressed about leaving the rest open
the first night booked, the rest left to figure out on the road
a one-way ticket and a vague idea — let the road decide

questions.scenario: Your desk or kitchen, honestly, is —

options.label: usually in order — and if someone moves a thing on it, I cannot relax until it is back
mostly tidy, with one small pile I am willing to defend — though I do judge other people's mess more than I admit
a working mess, with corners I clear when I cannot stand it
a creative mess — I mostly know where everything is and that is enough

questions.scenario: A deadline is two weeks out. You —

options.label: start early and finish early — the open task nags at me until it is closed
sketch a plan in week one, execute in week two
let it simmer, start in earnest with five or six days to go
feel the spark closer to the deadline — pressure clarifies what actually matters

questions.scenario: A plan you made gets disrupted at the last minute. Your honest first feeling is —

options.label: real irritation — I built the plan for a reason and now I have to rebuild it
mild annoyance, then I regroup and replan
a flicker of curiosity — okay, what is the new shape of the day?
a small thrill — what else is now possible?

questions.scenario: When a friend asks "want to do something tonight?", your default leans —

options.label: "let me check my calendar" — I genuinely prefer to know
"probably — let me look at what I had planned"
"sure, sounds fun" — I rarely say no to a last-minute plan, even when I probably should
"sure, what did you have in mind?" — open is genuinely my favourite way

questions.scenario: For you, a list of half-finished projects is mostly —

options.label: a quiet source of stress — I would rather close them out before starting anything new
a small weight I plan to clear, on principle
a living garden I tend in seasons, with one or two I keep meaning to finish
a garden of options — I will return to each in its season
